summary: The processing of the Amharic batch involved several key steps. PROTOCOL 1 (The Splitter) was applied to separate suffixes like 'ም' and 'ና' from their base words, specifically in 'ከሚትጐለጐልም' (split into 'ከሚትጐለጐል' and 'ም'), 'ከሚችሉም' (into 'ከሚችሉ' and 'ም'), 'ከሚኒስትርነትም' (into 'ከሚኒስትርነት' and 'ም'), 'ከሚናገሩም' (into 'ከሚናገሩ' and 'ም'), 'ከሚንቀሳቀሰውም' (into 'ከሚንቀሳቀሰው' and 'ም'), 'ከሚንቦገቦገውም' (into 'ከሚንቦገቦገው' and 'ም'), and 'ከሚኖሩትና' (into 'ከሚኖሩት' and 'ና'). PROTOCOL 2 (The Judge) resulted in no discards, as all input strings could be reasonably interpreted or corrected. PROTOCOL 3 (The Corrector) involved scholarly guesses for 'ከሚትቃም', which was corrected to 'ከሚጠቀም' (from the one who uses), assuming 'ት' was an OCR error for 'ጥ'. Similarly, 'ከሚትጐለጐልም' was corrected to 'ከሚጐለጐል' (from the one who is rolled), removing the extraneous 'ት'. 'ከሚንስትሯ' was corrected to 'ከሚኒስትሯ' (from her minister), addressing a common OCR confusion between 'ን' and 'ኒ'. PROTOCOL 4 (The Lemmatizer) focused on deriving the infinitive form for verbs (e.g., 'ማወቅ', 'መቻል', 'መኖር') and the base noun for nominalized forms (e.g., 'ሚኒስትር'). PROTOCOL 6 (The Grammarian) primarily categorized words as Nouns or Adverbs, reflecting the 'ከሚ-' prefix which often forms relative clauses functioning as nominals or adverbials of place/manner. PROTOCOL 7 (The Filter) ensured all resulting roots were at least two characters long, with no single-character words being included. The 'importance' scores were assigned based on the general utility and commonality of the words.

The batch consisted of 30 Amharic words, all starting with the preposition 'ከ' (from/than) followed by a relative clause verbal form. No words required splitting (Protocol 1) or were deemed semantically meaningless (Protocol 2). No obvious OCR errors or punctuation needed correction (Protocol 3). The primary task involved lemmatization (Protocol 4) to identify the infinitive root for each verb. For 'ከሚያንማር', the root 'መማር' (to learn) was identified as a scholarly guess, interpreting the 'ያን' as part of the relative clause structure despite its less common occurrence with this specific verb. Comprehensive English synonyms (Protocol 5) were provided, and each root was tagged as a 'Verb' (Protocol 6). All words and their roots met the minimum length requirement (Protocol 7). The 'importance' score was uniformly set to 7, reflecting their common usage.

Processed 30 Amharic strings. No words required splitting (PROTOCOL 1). Four words were discarded under PROTOCOL 2 (The Judge) due to being semantically meaningless or highly ambiguous without further context: 'ከማሖል', 'ከማምጠቅ', 'ከማራም', and 'ከማሴ'. These words could not be reliably corrected or identified as standard Amharic dictionary entries or common proper nouns. No minor OCR errors were identified for correction (PROTOCOL 3). For all valid words, the base dictionary entry (infinitive or noun form) was identified (PROTOCOL 4), comprehensive English synonyms were provided (PROTOCOL 5), and a single high-level part of speech was assigned (PROTOCOL 6). All included words met the minimum length requirement (PROTOCOL 7). Proper nouns like 'ከማሙሽ' and 'ከማርቪን' were identified and treated as nouns with themselves as the root.

summary: The batch contained 30 Amharic strings. One string, 'ከማውቀውና', was identified as a merged word and successfully split into 'ከማውቀው' and 'ና' (Protocol 1). All other strings were processed as single words. No words were discarded as semantic nonsense (Protocol 2) or due to unfixable ambiguity after minor OCR correction attempts (Protocol 3), as all were meaningful and correctly formed. Punctuation was not present in the input. For each valid word, the appropriate Amharic infinitive or base noun/pronoun was identified as the root (Protocol 4). Comprehensive English synonyms were provided (Protocol 5). Each word was assigned a high-level part of speech (Protocol 6). All words and their identified roots met the minimum two-character length requirement (Protocol 7). Proper nouns were treated as their own roots. Verb forms with prefixes like 'ከማ-' and suffixes were correctly lemmatized to their infinitive forms. The conjunction 'ና' was correctly identified and processed.

summary: The batch was processed through 8 scholarly protocols. PROTOCOL 1 (The Splitter) identified and separated the conjunction 'ና' from 'ከማይቀበሉአችሁና' and 'ከማይክሮሶፍትና'. These single-character 'ና' conjunctions were subsequently discarded by PROTOCOL 7 (The Filter) due to their length. PROTOCOL 2 (The Judge) led to the discarding of 'ከማይናማር' as it was deemed semantic nonsense in Amharic, likely a foreign proper noun that does not conform to Amharic morphology. PROTOCOL 3 (The Corrector) was applied to 'ከማይከሮሶፍት', which was corrected to 'ከማይክሮሶፍት' based on common knowledge of the proper noun 'Microsoft'. All other words were successfully lemmatized (PROTOCOL 4), translated (PROTOCOL 5), and tagged with their high-level part of speech (PROTOCOL 6) as either Verb or Noun, ensuring adherence to the strict categories. All remaining words and their roots met the minimum length requirement of PROTOCOL 7. The 'ም' suffix (meaning 'also' or 'even') was treated as an integral part of the word and not split, as it is a common Amharic enclitic.

The batch of Amharic words, primarily relative clauses of verbs, underwent an 8-protocol scholarly analysis. PROTOCOL 1 (The Splitter) identified and separated suffixes like 'ና' (and) and 'ም' (also) from 'ከሚጠሉኝና', 'ከሚጣፍጠውም', and 'ከሚፅፉና', ensuring only the core verbal phrase was processed. PROTOCOL 3 (The Corrector) addressed minor OCR inconsistencies, specifically correcting 'ከሚጋፉአችሁም' to 'ከሚጋፏችሁም' for better morphological accuracy and updating 'ከሚፅፉና' to 'ከሚጽፉና' to reflect modern Amharic spelling ('ጽ' instead of 'ፅ'). PROTOCOL 2 (The Judge) found all words to be semantically meaningful, thus no discards were made due to nonsense. PROTOCOL 4 (The Lemmatizer) successfully extracted the infinitive (መነሻ ቃል) for each verb, such as 'መጋፋት' from 'ከሚጋፏችሁም' and 'መጻፍ' from 'ከሚጽፉ'. PROTOCOL 6 (The Grammarian) consistently categorized all roots as 'Verb'. PROTOCOL 7 (The Filter) confirmed all cleaned words and their roots met the minimum two-character length requirement. PROTOCOL 5 (The Translator) provided comprehensive English synonyms for each root. The 'importance' scores were assigned based on the general utility and frequency of the verbs.

summary: The batch of Amharic words was processed through all eight scholarly protocols. No words required splitting as they were already correctly segmented. All words were semantically meaningful and successfully lemmatized to their infinitive forms, thus no words were discarded. One minor OCR correction was applied to 'ከሚድያውም', where 'ድ' was corrected to 'ዲ' to reflect the standard spelling of 'ሚዲያ' (media). The part of speech was assigned based on the primary function of the root word. All identified roots met the minimum length requirement of two characters. Importance scores were assigned with a default of 7 for verbs and 8 for nouns.

summary: The batch of Amharic words, all prefixed with 'ከ' (from), underwent rigorous processing. No merged words were identified by PROTOCOL 1. PROTOCOL 2 (Semantic Filtering) found all words to be semantically valid or correctable, thus no discards were made. PROTOCOL 3 (The Corrector) was applied to 'ከማቀርበዉ', which was corrected to 'ከማቀርበው' due to a common OCR error of 'ዉ' for 'ው'. Similarly, 'ከማቋቁም' was corrected to 'ከማቋቋም' to reflect the standard infinitive form. 'ከማናቸይቱም' was recognized as a grammatically specific, albeit less common, form meaning 'from any (feminine)', and not an OCR error. PROTOCOL 4 (The Lemmatizer) successfully identified the infinitive roots for all words, carefully distinguishing between direct infinitives and nominalized relative clauses (e.g., 'ከማትረሳቸው' rooted in 'መረስ'). PROTOCOL 5 (The Translator) provided comprehensive English synonyms. PROTOCOL 6 (The Grammarian) assigned 'Noun' to most entries, as the 'ከ' prefix often nominalizes the subsequent verb or noun phrase, and 'Pronoun' for 'ከማናቸይቱም'. PROTOCOL 7 (The Filter) confirmed all words and roots met the minimum two-character length requirement. All protocols were strictly adhered to.

The batch was processed through all 8 scholarly protocols. Several words required splitting due to merged conjunctions ('ና') or adverbs ('ም'). For instance, 'ከማጭበርበርና' was split into 'ከማጭበርበር' and 'ና', and 'ከምሕረትህና' into 'ከምሕረትህ' and 'ና'. Similarly, words ending with 'ም' (also/even) like 'ከማጽናናትዋም', 'ከምሥረታውም', 'ከምሥጢርም', 'ከምረውም', 'ከምሬትም', and 'ከምርጦቻቸውም' were split to isolate the adverbial suffix. All identified words were semantically meaningful, and no entries were discarded due to nonsense or uncorrectable errors. Minor punctuation stripping was applied implicitly where necessary (e.g., if '::' were present). Lemmatization focused on identifying the infinitive for verbs (e.g., 'ከማጠን' -> 'ማጠን', 'ከምመለስ' -> 'መመለስ') and the base noun/adjective for others (e.g., 'ከማፊያዎች' -> 'ማፊያ', 'ከምሕረትህ' -> 'ምሕረት'). A scholarly guess was made for 'ከምረው' where, after careful consideration of potential noun roots like 'ምርጫ' (choice) and verb roots like 'መመረር' (to be bitter) or 'መመርመር' (to examine), 'መምረጥ' (to choose/select) was chosen as the most plausible infinitive root, implying 'from what he chose'. All words and roots met the minimum two-character length requirement. Part-of-speech tagging was assigned at a high level as per Protocol 6.

summary: The input batch was processed through 8 scholarly protocols. Several words required splitting due to merged suffixes, specifically the conjunction 'ና' ('and') and the adverbial suffix 'ም' ('also/even'). The single-character suffix 'ም' was consistently discarded as per Protocol 7 (Size Constraint), which mandates a minimum of two characters for inclusion. Lemmatization involved identifying the infinitive form for verbs (e.g., 'ማዳመጥ' for 'ከምናደምጣቸው') and the base noun or adjective for others (e.g., 'ምኞት' for 'ከምኞትዋ', 'ምዕራባዊ' for 'ከምዕራባዊው'). For 'ከምዋቹ', 'መዋሸት' (to lie) was chosen as the most appropriate root. Part of speech tagging adhered strictly to the high-level categories specified in Protocol 6. No semantic nonsense was found, and no minor OCR errors required correction beyond stripping punctuation (which was not present in this batch).
